instructions:
This is the reading and grammar section of the MET.

There are three parts in this section, with special directions for each part.

Try to answer all the questions. If you are not sure about an answer, you may guess.

You may answer the questions in any order you wish. You may click Back to go back to the last question. The numbers on the side of the screen will show you which questions have been answered.

parts.instructions: In this part of the test, you will answer some grammar questions. Choose the word or phrase to complete each sentence from the choices given.

questions.question: If the committee _______ the proposal earlier, the project could have started last month.
The manager suggested that the interns _______ more time on research before presenting.
Rarely _______ such a convincing argument presented in class.
The new regulations require that all employees _______ safety training annually.
No sooner _______ the announcement made than the audience began to applaud.
The director insisted that the report _______ in greater detail before being published.
By the time the conference began, the participants _______ all the necessary documents.
options: had been prepared every required file
had to prepare every required file

questions.question: The new policy aims to ensure that employees _______ equal opportunities regardless of background.
The teacher reminded the students that plagiarism _______ serious consequences in academic work.
options: could have resulted in severe penalties
could be result in severe penalties

questions.question: The project was delayed because the team _______ the technical issues earlier.
options: had not been addressed them properly
had not to address them properly

questions.question: The students decided to organize a meeting _______ they could discuss the project together.
options: so that they could share ideas

questions.question: The teacher asked the class to read the article carefully _______ they would understand the topic better.
options: because they would gain more knowledge
because they to gain more knowledge

questions.question: The company offered training sessions _______ the employees could improve their computer skills.
options: so that they could learn faster

questions.question: The children were excited to go to the park _______ they could play with their friends.
options: because they wanted to have fun
because they want to have fun
because they wanting to have fun

questions.question: The manager explained the new rules clearly _______ everyone could follow them without confusion.
options: so that they could understand easily

questions.question: The students were late _______.
The teacher asked us _______.
The company will hire _______.
The children enjoyed _______.
The meeting was canceled _______.

parts.instructions: In this part of the test, you will read longer texts and answer some questions about them. Choose the answer to each question from the choices given.

questions.passage:
Around the world, renewable energy has become a central topic in discussions about the future of our planet. Unlike fossil fuels, which release large amounts of carbon dioxide and contribute to climate change, renewable sources such as solar, wind, and hydroelectric power generate electricity without producing harmful emissions. Solar panels can be installed on rooftops, providing clean energy directly to households. Wind turbines, often located in rural or coastal areas, transform the power of moving air into electricity. Hydroelectric dams use flowing water to produce energy, and in some countries they already supply a significant portion of national electricity needs.

One of the greatest advantages of renewable energy is sustainability. Fossil fuels are limited and will eventually run out, but sunlight, wind, and water are naturally replenished. Another benefit is economic: once the infrastructure is built, the cost of producing renewable energy is relatively low compared to traditional methods. However, there are challenges. Solar panels depend on weather conditions and produce less energy on cloudy days. Wind turbines require specific geographic locations, and hydroelectric dams can affect local ecosystems. Despite these obstacles, governments and private companies continue to invest heavily in renewable technologies. Experts believe that renewable energy will play a decisive role in reducing pollution and ensuring a cleaner, healthier future for generations to come.
questions.question: What is one main difference between fossil fuels and renewable energy?
options: Fossil fuels are cheaper to install

questions.passage:
Urbanization refers to the increasing number of people living in cities rather than rural areas. Over the past century, this trend has accelerated due to industrial growth, better job opportunities, and improved living standards in urban centers. Cities often provide access to modern healthcare, education, and transportation, which attract families seeking a higher quality of life. In addition, urban areas are cultural hubs, offering museums, theaters, and diverse restaurants that enrich social experiences.

However, rapid urbanization also creates challenges. Overcrowding can lead to housing shortages and higher living costs. Traffic congestion and pollution are common problems in large cities, affecting both health and productivity. Furthermore, the expansion of cities sometimes reduces green spaces, which are essential for recreation and environmental balance. Governments and city planners are working to address these issues by promoting sustainable development, such as building affordable housing, improving public transportation, and creating parks. Experts believe that urbanization will continue to grow, but its success depends on careful planning to ensure that cities remain healthy and livable for future generations.
questions.question: What is one main reason people move to cities?

parts.instructions: In this part of the test, you will read three shorter texts (journalist style) about a similar topic and answer some questions about them. Choose the answer to each question from the choices given.

article.title: The Rise of Electric Vehicles
article.content:
Cleaner streets, quieter engines, and a vision of sustainable mobility are driving the rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) across the globe. Once considered experimental, EVs are now entering the mainstream, reshaping the way societies think about transportation.

• More than 10 million EVs are already on the road worldwide, with sales increasing each year.
• Advances in battery technology allow longer trips and faster charging times.
• Governments encourage adoption through tax incentives and the expansion of charging networks.
• Automakers invest heavily in research, producing models that combine efficiency, safety, and style.
• Environmental benefits include reduced air pollution and lower greenhouse gas emissions.

Challenges remain. Charging infrastructure is uneven, and battery production depends on rare minerals that raise sustainability concerns. Yet experts agree that the momentum is unstoppable.

For many citizens, the electric car is more than a vehicle—it is a symbol of progress. As cities adapt and industries innovate, EVs stand as a promise of cleaner air, smarter mobility, and a future where technology and responsibility travel side by side.

questions.question: What is one environmental benefit of electric vehicles mentioned in the article?
What challenge related to electric vehicles is highlighted in the article?
According to the article, what concern is linked to battery production?

article.title: Interview with Daniel Brooks on Nikola Tesla
article.subheading: Midvillage Daily News staff reporter Helen Carter interviews automotive historian Daniel Brooks.
article.content:
Q: In your view, who has been the most influential figure in the history of electric vehicles?

A: Without hesitation, I would say Nikola Tesla. His vision of harnessing electricity for practical use laid the foundation for technologies that inspire today's engineers. Though he lived in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, his ideas remain alive in every modern battery and motor.

Q: Could you tell us about his early life?

A: Tesla was born in 1856 in what is now Croatia. From a young age, he showed remarkable talent for mathematics and imagination. He often spoke of vivid dreams and inventions that came to him like flashes of light. That creative energy never left him.

Q: How did he begin his career in the field of electricity?

A: He moved to the United States in 1884 and quickly found work with Thomas Edison. Their collaboration was short-lived, but it gave Tesla the chance to demonstrate his brilliance. Soon after, he developed the alternating current system, which became the backbone of modern power distribution.

Q: What would you say was his greatest contribution to the world of technology?

A: Tesla's gift was his ability to see beyond the present. His alternating current system changed the way electricity was delivered, making it possible to power homes and industries across vast distances. More than that, his passion for innovation continues to inspire the engineers who design the electric vehicles of today.

questions.question: According to the interview, what was Tesla's greatest contribution?
Where did Tesla begin his career in the United States?
What inspired Tesla's lifelong passion for invention?
In which year did Tesla move to the United States?

article.title: The Value of Using Technology in Education
article.author: Dr. Laura Mitchell
article.bio: Dr. Laura Mitchell is a professor of educational technology and consultant for innovative schools.
article.content:
In classrooms across the world, technology has become more than a tool—it is a bridge between students and knowledge. From interactive whiteboards to online platforms, digital resources are transforming the way young learners and adolescents engage with lessons. Properly used, technology does not distract; it empowers.

Digital media captures attention and sparks imagination. A video clip can illustrate a scientific concept more vividly than a textbook, while interactive apps encourage students to practice skills with enthusiasm. Technology also promotes values and life skills: collaboration in group projects, responsibility in managing online tasks, and awareness of global issues through instant access to information.

Older students, already immersed in digital culture, find comfort in learning through familiar platforms. Lessons delivered with multimedia are often more relevant, current, and engaging. Teachers who embrace technology are perceived as approachable and modern, strengthening the relationship between instructor and student. This bond, in turn, enhances retention and deepens understanding.

Technology in education is not a passing trend. It is a powerful ally that enriches learning, encourages critical thinking, and prepares students for a world where digital literacy is as essential as reading and writing.

questions.question: What is one advantage of using technology in classrooms mentioned in the article?
options: It eliminates the need for textbooks

questions.question: What is one way technology helps older students, according to the article?
options: It reduces their interest in lessons
It connects them through familiar platforms
It eliminates the need for teachers
It limits their access to information

questions.question: What common theme unites the three readings?
options: The role of innovation in shaping society

article.title: The Spirit of Local Markets
article.content:
Colorful stalls, lively voices, and the aroma of fresh food fill the air in local markets across the world. Once seen only as places to buy daily necessities, these markets are now celebrated as cultural landmarks, attracting visitors eager to experience authentic traditions.

• Local markets showcase regional foods, crafts, and handmade goods.
• They provide economic opportunities for small producers and artisans.
• Tourists discover cultural heritage through flavors, colors, and conversations.
• Markets strengthen community ties by bringing people together in shared spaces.
• Sustainability is promoted through local sourcing and reduced transport.

Challenges exist, of course. Modern supermarkets and online shopping compete with traditional markets, and maintaining hygiene and infrastructure requires effort. Yet the resilience of these spaces is undeniable.

For many communities, the market is more than commerce—it is a living stage where culture, identity, and human connection thrive. As cities grow and societies change, local markets remain a timeless reminder that tradition and community are treasures worth preserving.

questions.question: What is one cultural benefit of local markets mentioned in the article?
What challenge do local markets face according to the article?
options: Competition from supermarkets and online shopping

questions.question: What sustainability benefit is associated with local markets?

article.title: The Evolution of Public Libraries
article.author: Prof. Eleanor Grant
article.content:
Early Period (1830–1850) — Public libraries began as small collections funded by local communities and philanthropists. Their purpose was simple: to provide access to books for those who could not afford them. In these early years, libraries were modest, often housed in single rooms, but they represented a revolutionary idea—knowledge for all.

Middle Period (1850–1900) — With industrial growth and rising literacy rates, libraries expanded rapidly. Many cities built grand library buildings, often in neoclassical or Victorian styles. Collections grew to include newspapers, journals, and reference works. The library became not only a place to read but also a civic symbol of progress and education.

Late Period (1900–1950) — Libraries embraced new technologies, such as card catalogs and lending systems, making access easier. They also began offering lectures, exhibitions, and children's programs. By mid-century, the public library had become a cornerstone of community life, adapting to social changes while preserving its mission of free access to knowledge.

Today, libraries continue to evolve with digital resources, online catalogs, and multimedia collections. Yet their essence remains unchanged: they are spaces where learning, culture, and community converge.

questions.question: In the early period (1830–1850), what was the main purpose of public libraries?
options: To provide access to books for those who could not afford them

questions.question: During the middle period (1850–1900), what symbol did libraries represent?
options: A civic symbol of progress and education

questions.question: What innovation did libraries adopt in the early 20th century?
By mid-century, what role had libraries assumed in community life?
options: They became centers of political debate
They were cornerstones of community life
They focused only on children's programs

article.title: Voices of Tradition: The Story of Local Artisans
article.author: Dr. Samuel Hayes
article.bio: Dr. Samuel Hayes is a cultural historian and lecturer specializing in traditional crafts and community heritage.
article.content:
In every bustling market, behind the colorful stalls and handcrafted goods, there are artisans whose stories embody resilience and creativity. These men and women carry forward traditions that have been passed down through generations, shaping not only objects but also cultural identity.

Born into families of weavers, potters, and woodcarvers, many artisans begin their craft in childhood. Their skills are honed through practice, patience, and a deep respect for materials. Each piece they create—whether a woven basket, a ceramic vase, or a carved figure—tells a story of heritage and community.

The challenges are real. Globalization and mass production often overshadow handmade goods, and younger generations sometimes turn away from traditional crafts. Yet artisans continue to adapt, using modern platforms to share their work with wider audiences. Markets, fairs, and cultural festivals provide spaces where their voices can be heard and their creations valued.

For Dr. Hayes, these artisans are more than producers of goods; they are guardians of memory. Their crafts remind us that culture is not static but alive, evolving with each generation while holding firm to its roots.

questions.question: According to the article, what challenge do artisans face today?
options: Overshadowing by globalization and mass production

questions.question: What do artisans' crafts symbolize according to Dr. Hayes?
